questions.question: Describe the Chain of Justice introduced by Jahangir.
questions.answer: The Chain of Justice was a golden chain with bells that Emperor Jahangir hung outside his palace soon after ascending the Mughal throne. Any subject who had a grievance could come and ring the bells on this chain to seek justice directly from the emperor. This showed Jahangir's desire to be a benevolent and accessible ruler. The Chain of Justice symbolised his commitment to hearing the complaints of even the humblest of his subjects without having to go through corrupt officials or intermediaries.

questions.question: What was the significance of the execution of Guru Arjan Dev by Jahangir?
questions.answer: The fifth Sikh Guru, Arjan Dev, had given shelter to Prince Khusrau at Taran Taran during his rebellion against Jahangir in 1606. As punishment, Jahangir had Guru Arjan Dev executed. This was an unwise political decision by Jahangir because it sowed the seeds of a long and drawn-out struggle between the Mughals and the Sikhs. The enmity that began with this execution continued through Aurangzeb's reign and contributed to the decline of Mughal power.

questions.question: How did the British East India Company establish trade in India during Jahangir's reign?
questions.answer: The East India Company received its royal charter from Queen Elizabeth I on December 31, 1600. In 1608, the Company sent Captain William Hawkins to Jahangir's court to secure royal patronage, and he succeeded in getting permits for factories at various places on the western coast. In 1615, Sir Thomas Roe was sent by Emperor James I of England for more concessions. Roe was very diplomatic and secured a royal charter for the Company to trade freely in the whole Mughal territory. This laid the foundation for British commercial presence in India.

questions.question: What was the role of Nur Jahan during Jahangir's reign?
questions.answer: Nur Jahan was a woman of remarkable strength and courage who wielded great power during Jahangir's reign. She often presided over the court alongside Jahangir and took part in matters of state. Due to Jahangir's addiction to opium and alcohol, he was often absent from court, making it easier for Nur Jahan to assert her authority. She was the only Mughal empress who had coins minted with her name. She was known for her shrewd planning and physical strength, and rescued Jahangir from the clutches of rebel leader Mahabat Khan in 1626.

questions.question: How did Shah Jahan deal with the Portuguese in Bengal?
questions.answer: The Portuguese had established a factory at Hugli in Bengal and were found to be lifting orphaned children and converting them to Christianity. This was objectionable to Shah Jahan as a devout Muslim. In 1631, Shah Jahan appointed Qasim Khan as the Governor of Bengal and gave him authority to teach the Portuguese a lesson. The Portuguese were attacked and massacred, and Hugli was besieged for three months. Through this decisive action, Shah Jahan gave the Portuguese a death blow in Bengal and ended their wrongful activities.
questions.source_section: Shah Jahan - Policy Towards the Portuguese

questions.question: What were the circumstances of the war of succession after Shah Jahan fell ill?
questions.answer: Shah Jahan had nominated his eldest son Dara Shikoh as his heir, but there was no fixed system of primogeniture in the Mughal Empire. When Shah Jahan became ill, a tussle for power broke out among his four sons. Aurangzeb defeated Dara Shikoh's forces at the battles of Dharmat and Samugarh in 1657 and 1658. After seizing the throne, Aurangzeb imprisoned his father Shah Jahan, killed his brothers Dara and Murad, while Shuja ran away. Shah Jahan remained in prison for eight years at Agra Fort until his death in 1666.

questions.question: Describe the conflict between Aurangzeb and the Sikhs.
questions.answer: The conflict between the Mughals and Sikhs had begun when Jahangir executed the fifth Guru Arjan Dev. Under Aurangzeb, it intensified when the ninth Guru Tegh Bahadur revolted after Aurangzeb ordered the destruction of Sikh gurudwaras. Guru Tegh Bahadur was captured, taken to Delhi, asked to embrace Islam, and upon refusal was executed in December 1675 in Chandni Chowk. His son, the tenth Guru Gobind Singh, organised the Sikhs into the Khalsa, a powerful military force. Fierce conflicts followed, and Guru Gobind Singh lost all four of his sons to the Mughals.

questions.question: How did Shivaji establish the Maratha kingdom?
questions.answer: Shivaji captured the principality of Javli in 1656 and raided Bijapur territory. When the Sultan of Bijapur sent general Afzal Khan to capture him in 1659, Shivaji killed him. By 1662, the Sultan of Bijapur acknowledged Shivaji as an independent ruler. Though forced to sign the Treaty of Purandhar in 1665 and taken prisoner at Agra, Shivaji escaped to Raigarh in 1666. He reconquered his surrendered forts and in 1674, crowned himself the Maratha king at Raigarh, taking the title of Chhatrapati. He died in 1680 after founding the Maratha kingdom.
questions.source_section: Rise of the Marathas and Chhatrapati Shivaji

questions.question: What were the architectural features of Shah Jahan's buildings?
questions.answer: Shah Jahan's architectural style was characterised by several distinct features. He replaced red stone with white marble as the primary building material. His buildings emphasised symmetry and uniformity in their design. He introduced the use of the double dome, and the technique of placing one arch within another. The pietra dura or parelinkari method of decoration involved inlaying semi-precious stones into marble to create floral designs. His buildings were also adorned with floral motifs and calligraphic verses from the holy Quran.

questions.question: What was the loss of Kandahar and why was it significant?
questions.answer: Kandahar was a bone of contention between the Mughals and the Persians due to its strategic location. Shah Abbas of Persia captured Kandahar from the Mughals in June 1622 during Jahangir's reign, dealing a severe blow to Mughal power in Central Asia. Shah Jahan recovered Kandahar in 1638, but the Shah of Iran captured it again in 1649. Despite three major campaigns, the Mughals could not recover Kandahar. This loss demonstrated the declining military strength of the Mughal Empire and its inability to protect its north-western frontier.

questions.question: Why is Aurangzeb considered responsible for the decline of the Mughal Empire?
questions.answer: Aurangzeb's suspicious nature prevented his sons and nobles from becoming capable rulers. His spiritual bigotry caused revolts by the Jats, Satnamis, and Sikhs, while his annexation of Marwar caused a serious rift in Mughal-Rajput relations. He reimposed the jizya tax and destroyed Hindu temples, losing the loyalty of Hindus. His Deccan campaign lasted twenty-six years and drained the empire's resources. The constant attention to the Deccan left the north unattended, allowing revolts and regions to assert independence. His rigid policies turned the Rajputs, Jats, Sikhs, and Marathas all into enemies.

questions.question: What were Aurangzeb's religious policies and their impact?
questions.answer: Aurangzeb aimed to establish Sunni religion throughout India. He banned music, dancing, and wine in the royal court. He deprived Rajputs of higher posts and prohibited Hindu festivals like Diwali. He discontinued the practice of jharoka darshan. He reimposed the jizya and pilgrim tax on Hindus. He restricted Hindus from possessing arms, horses, elephants, and palanquins. He ordered the destruction of temples like the Vishwanath temple at Banaras and erected mosques in their place. These rigid measures invited widespread revolts and paved the way for the decline of the Mughal Empire.

questions.question: Describe the important contributions of the Mughals to painting.
questions.answer: Under Humayun, one of the first Mughal paintings was 'Princes of the House of Timur', and Persian artists Abd-al-Samad and Mir Sayyid Ali were brought to the court. Under Akbar, the Tutinama (Tales of a Parrot) was illustrated. Mughal painting reached its greatest heights under Jahangir, who introduced the element of perspective influenced by European style. Portraits and studies of birds, flowers, and animals became common themes. Famous painters included Bishan Das, Daulat, Kanha, and Shankar. Shah Jahan continued patronising painting, but under Aurangzeb painting declined and painters migrated to regional kingdoms.

questions.question: What were the various revolts during Aurangzeb's reign?
questions.answer: Aurangzeb's reign was marked by numerous revolts. In 1669-1670, the Jats rose in the Mathura region under the leadership of Gokula. The Bundelas under Champati Rai and Chhatrasal revolted in Bundelkhand. The Satnamis revolted due to agrarian discontent and Aurangzeb's rigid policies. The Sikhs revolted under Guru Tegh Bahadur and later under Guru Gobind Singh. The Mughal-Rajput relations deteriorated after the annexation of Marwar. Most significantly, the Marathas under Chhatrapati Shivaji established their own kingdom in the Deccan and challenged Mughal authority.

questions.question: Describe the Shalimar Bagh and its history.
questions.answer: The Shalimar Bagh is a famous Mughal garden in Kashmir. Emperor Jahangir enlarged an ancient garden in 1619 and called it 'Farah Baksh' meaning 'the delightful', creating it for his wife Nur Jahan ('Light of the World'). Later in 1630, under Shah Jahan's orders, Zafar Khan, the governor of Kashmir, extended the garden further and named the new section 'Faiz Baksh' meaning 'the bountiful'. The garden is a fine example of the famous Mughal gardens that Jahangir was known for laying out in Kashmir.
